## [00:00 - 03:01]

Okay guys, so I'm going to take you through in the next videos that are coming up in this module targeting. This is a meaty section and it is often the section most Facebook marketers get strung up on. So here's how I like to think of targeting. It's not saying this is the perfect way or the exact way, but this is how I like to think about targeting on Facebook ads. So we've broken it down into three segments. We've essentially gone from cold to hot. Now for those of you who are not familiar with that sort of descriptive or adjective for an audience, essentially all it means is a cold audience is someone who has never interacted with your brand or your products or your Facebook page. In many cases they don't even know who you are. They're totally cold. All the way through to a hot audience, which is someone that's in this situation has visited a sales page. So that could be a sales page for a program. It could be a sales page for e-commerce, whatever that might be. So this is how we're going to break down targeting in the upcoming training. So just so you guys are aware, cold audiences are obviously people that have never engaged with you. So that's where you're using your interests, your demographics, behavioral targeting. For example, they're interested in surfing or they've recently went on holiday to some place or their females aged 18 to 24. Those are demographics, behavioral and interest based targeting. This is cold traffic, right? They've never really heard of you before. And lookalikes also fall under here. Now a lookalike is an audience that you build from a custom audience, from an existing audience. So that might be an email list. It might be website visitors. And you're essentially telling Facebook to create an audience that looks very similar to your existing audience of, for example, customers. However, Facebook will exclude that initial audience. Your customer list or your website visitors, meaning that a lookalike is actually made up of cold traffic, of a cold audience, people that have never really engaged with you before. Now in the middle, what we have is warm. Now this is typically going to be people that viewed videos. Maybe they like your Facebook page. Maybe they've engaged with ads or page posts. Maybe they visited your website or blog posts. They're warmed up. They know who you are. They've engaged with you. They've interacted with you. And then hot, as mentioned already, has visited sales pages. So a couple distinctions that are really important here, guys.

## [03:01 - 06:03]

With cold targeting, your lookalike is a cold audience. Don't forget that. And likewise, with warm and hot audiences, these are what we call custom audiences. So you're going to hear me throw that word a lot in the next training videos, in the next few training videos. And this is essentially what I'm referring to. Custom audiences are essentially individuals that have interacted or engaged with your business, your products, your brand in some capacity. It could be as lightweight as they watch 10 seconds of a funny video you launched, as heavy as they purchase something from you, or they requested a quote. With your cold targeting, I'm going to be showing you exactly the options that you have here and some really cool hacks in terms of figuring out the right targeting for you and also how to create lookalikes. But just a few quick pointers here. Now I know that they typically recommend you have a really large audience on Facebook. So Facebook typically loves big audiences, especially if you're using OCPM. However, what we have found through a lot of trial and error is there seems to be a really, really sweet spot at the moment for audience sizes that are about 40,000. So actually very, very small in terms of Facebook terms. Now a point here, a caveat, I guess, is that these audiences are not very scalable. So if you're targeting an audience size of 40,000 and you're spending about $100 a day, you're going to tap that audience out or you're going to get ad fatigue in the space of about four weeks to six weeks. So that's something that's really important to note here. If you need scalability, especially over longer periods of time without having to constantly refresh creative, you want to opt for an audience size that's a little bit more sustainable at around $1 million. Now for warm and cold targeting, so people that have interacted with you or maybe they purchased from you, maybe they're customers, some of the options that you have here is retargeting these individuals or creating custom audiences based on engagement. So have they engaged with a page with a piece of content, frequency, the specific pages they visited on your website, and the time. And you can also under engagement create custom audiences of, for example, people that visited your website and were the top 25% engaged audience on your website. So they didn't bounce, for example. And one of the things here that we're going to look at in the next few videos is also

## [06:03 - 06:24]

creating retargeting stacks. So not just retargeting everyone that visited your website in the last 180 days with the same creative. That's a really great way to fail at retargeting.
